market_context: The March 2-13 period was defined by two sequential macro shocks that compounded bearish pressure. The week of March 2 opened with Trump reconfirming 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico plus 20% on China, effective March 4 — triggering an immediate S&P 500 drop of ~1.8% on Mar 3. Markets failed to find footing as the Feb jobs report on Mar 6 delivered a second gut-punch: -92,000 payrolls vs. -50,000 expected, the third payroll decline in five months, with unemployment rising to 4.4%. This dual shock of stagflation fears (tariff-driven inflation + weak labor) repriced the macro narrative sharply. The S&P 500 posted its third consecutive weekly loss for the first time in about a year. Tech saw mixed signals — AVGO reported exceptional Q1 earnings after the Mar 4 close (AI revenue +106% YoY, Q2 guide $22B) but broader sector was overwhelmed by macro. The Feb CPI on Mar 11 came in tame (2.4% YoY, in-line), briefly stabilizing sentiment, but Iran-war-linked oil price spikes stoked stagflation fears that offset the relief. By Mar 13, the Nasdaq was down -1.3% WTD and S&P -1.6% WTD. The dominant regime: risk-off, rate-cut odds advancing (July now base), energy prices rising. FOMC March meeting not until Mar 18 — pre-meeting communication was hawkish-on-hold. Small caps most vulnerable to tariff + weak employment combo. Energy was the clear beneficiary of Iran war premium.

trades.thesis: Trump confirms 25% tariffs on Canada/Mexico effective Mar 4 at 12:01am, adding 20% China tariff same day. Three major trading partners hit simultaneously — this is a regime shift, not a blip. Supply chains will be repriced, corporate margins will compress, consumer spending will weaken. S&P 500 already down ~1.8% on the day; the actual implementation overnight catalyzes further deterioration. FOMC constrained by tariff-driven inflation — no relief valve from rate cuts near-term.
trades.catalyst: 25%/20% tariffs confirmed effective Mar 4; Canada/China immediate retaliation signaled; S&P had already erased most Nov-2024 election gains
trades.lesson: Tariff regime confirmation — not mere threat — was the right short trigger. SPY continued lower through March, hitting ~632 at Mar 30 trough before partial recovery to 650. Exiting at 20-day mark captured bulk of the move before late-month bounce began.

trades.thesis: Tech specifically more vulnerable than SPY on tariff day: Nasdaq-100 fell 2.6% vs. S&P -1.8% on tariff confirmation. Tech supply chains (semiconductors, consumer electronics) disproportionately exposed to China and Taiwan tariff risk. DeepSeek narrative still lingering (AI capex payback uncertainty). With FOMC unable to cut (tariff inflation) and growth slowing, premium multiples in Nasdaq unjustifiable. Double-short (SPY + QQQ) captures sector-specific downside beta.
trades.catalyst: Nasdaq -2.6% on Mar 3 tariff day vs. S&P -1.8% — revealing elevated tech vulnerability; China tariff 20% directly hits semiconductor supply chain
trades.lesson: Tech-specific short worked alongside SPY short — QQQ bottomed at ~558 at Mar 30 then bounced to 577 by Mar 31. The March selloff was real and sustained. Both shorts were complementary trades capturing the same macro view with different beta.

trades.thesis: Broadcom reported Q1 FY26 after Mar 4 close: revenue $19.3B (+29% YoY), AI semiconductor revenue $8.4B (+106% YoY), Q2 guide of $22B implies +47% YoY. CEO guided AI chip revenue >$100B in 2027. These are exceptional numbers — best AI infrastructure story in semis. Stock rose only modestly in after-hours (~0.7%). Buying the Mar 5 open/close allows capturing the post-earnings AI re-rating on proven execution vs. NVDA narrative. Entry into macro weakness but earnings quality should offer downside protection.
trades.catalyst: Q1 FY26 beat: EPS $2.05 vs. $1.88 est (+9%); AI rev $8.4B +106% YoY; Q2 guide $22B; CEO $100B 2027 AI target
trades.lesson: Earnings quality was real but macro overwhelmed stock-specific catalyst. AVGO fell from 332 to 293 at Mar 30 trough as tariff + stagflation fears crushed all semis regardless of fundamentals. The lesson: in a regime-shift macro selloff, earnings beats don't protect — they just provide a better entry once the macro clears. Should have waited for the Mar 6 payrolls data before entering longs.

trades.thesis: Feb payrolls -92,000 (vs. -50K est), unemployment rises to 4.4% — third payroll decline in five months. Small caps most exposed to domestic credit/growth conditions: higher borrowing costs, tariff pass-through on inputs, weakening consumer. IWM already had high short float and tariff-sensitive sectors. Weak jobs = rate cuts delayed (tariff inflation) = no Fed put + shrinking economy = double squeeze on small caps. Weaker than expected domestic economy directly transmitted to IWM.
trades.catalyst: Feb NFP -92,000 vs -50,000 est; unemployment 4.4%; Dow fell 903 pts at open; IWM already down ~5% from Feb peak
trades.lesson: IWM short thesis was directionally correct (small caps weaker) but IWM bottomed at ~240 and recovered to 252 by Apr 6 exit. The market began pricing in more Fed cuts due to weak jobs, which actually supported IWM. Stagflation read was incomplete — the jobs weakness pulled forward cut expectations which partially offset the negative growth read for small caps.

trades.thesis: GOOGL down ~2.4% the prior week to 306, trading at reasonable ~20x forward P/E given AI integration across Search, Cloud, YouTube. Unlike pure-play hardware semis, GOOGL's tariff exposure is software/services-dominant — minimal direct supply chain impact. AI monetization cycle accelerating (Gemini integration, cloud AI growth). After three weeks of macro selloff, GOOGL's durable cash flows and buyback (~5% yield) provide floor. Bounce play on stabilization after the extreme fear of Mar 3-6 period.
trades.catalyst: Post-payroll stabilization; GOOGL valuation cheap vs peers on tariff-adjusted basis; limited direct tariff exposure vs hardware names
trades.lesson: Thesis not wrong but timing was early. GOOGL ground lower to ~274 in late March before recovering to 305 at exit. The stock did recover but the 20-day window ended almost exactly flat. GOOGL is a quality name but quality doesn't matter when systematic macro selling is ongoing. Should have waited for CPI data before initiating longs.

trades.thesis: TSLA uniquely vulnerable in current environment: (1) 25% Canada/Mexico tariffs disrupt supply chain for parts sourced from Mexico (80K+ jobs at Mexican suppliers); (2) EV subsidy rollback risk under Trump 2.0; (3) Musk political exposure creating brand damage in key markets (EU protests/boycotts reported); (4) TSLA at ~398 still trading at 80x+ trailing P/E with delivery growth stalling. Unlike NVDA which has secular AI demand floor, TSLA's story depends on execution + policy support. Stock is a sentiment proxy — stagflation environment kills the growth premium.
trades.catalyst: Tariff supply chain risk (Mexico parts); Musk brand damage in EU; EV subsidy risk; stock still richly valued after prior 2-week selloff
trades.lesson: Best short in the book. TSLA collapsed from 398 to 343 at the Apr 7 exit — all thesis elements played out simultaneously. The supply chain / Musk brand / premium valuation trifecta in a risk-off macro environment is a high-conviction setup. TSLA shorts work when the bull narrative (Musk execution + policy support) is structurally challenged.

trades.thesis: Iran war escalation causing oil price spikes — classic supply disruption premium. XOM is the cleanest expression: largest US E&P, integrated refining, low breakeven (~$45/bbl), $30B+ buyback ongoing. As oil rises, XOM earnings estimates jump disproportionately due to operating leverage. Importantly, XOM provides inflation hedge in a stagflation scenario where equities (especially tech) suffer. Sector rotation from growth to energy is the natural portfolio response. Iran war risk is structural for the period, not fleeting.
trades.catalyst: Iran war oil price spike; XOM was actually down YTD vs. rising oil — presenting divergence opportunity; stagflation fears increasing energy allocation
trades.lesson: Geopolitical oil premium played out as expected — XOM rose from 147 to 168 at peak (Mar 31) before pulling back to 155 at exit. The Iran war thesis provided a durable tailwind. Energy longs in stagflation environments with a geopolitical bid are reliable — the sector acts as the natural hedge for the portfolio of shorts in growth/tech.

trades.thesis: Feb CPI released today came in at 2.4% YoY — in line with expectations — but this is the trough. Forward-looking, Iran war oil prices will push March/April CPI higher. Traders focused on forward inflation, not backward CPI. With tariffs adding structural inflation pressure AND oil spiking, the Fed's path to cuts is blocked. TLT (20yr Treasury) faces: (1) inflation premium rising, (2) fiscal deficit concerns unchanged, (3) any economic weakness met with more borrowing not lower yields. TLT at 86-87 looks extended given actual macro backdrop.
trades.catalyst: Feb CPI in-line at 2.4% but Iran oil spike signals forward CPI acceleration; tariff inflation structural; Fed hold confirmed at March FOMC
trades.lesson: TLT short thesis was correct directionally — forward CPI did rise, March FOMC held — but TLT oscillated in a narrow range (86-88) during the period as weak growth signals offset inflation fears. The competing narratives (inflation bad for bonds vs. growth fears good for bonds) created a range-bound outcome. TLT required a cleaner setup — either unambiguous growth strength or inflation alone. The stagflation ambiguity neutralized the trade.

trades.thesis: AMD at 197 after a 20%+ decline from Jan peaks — valuation now compelling at ~25x forward earnings. AMD's MI300X gaining enterprise adoption as an NVIDIA alternative, with hyperscalers actively qualifying AMD for AI inference workloads. Data center revenue tracking to grow >50% YoY in 2026. Unlike consumer semis, AMD data center exposure has minimal tariff sensitivity (software/IP driven, not import-heavy hardware). With AVGO earnings confirming AI infrastructure spending is accelerating, AMD is the catch-up trade. Stock has digested the DeepSeek narrative; the efficiency-drives-more-demand dynamic (Jevons) supports continued capex.
trades.catalyst: AVGO earnings confirmed AI infrastructure spending acceleration; AMD at trough valuation vs peers; data center AI inference adoption growing
trades.lesson: Best long in the period — AMD surged from 197 to 245 in 20 trading days (+23.9%). The macro selloff had created a massive entry opportunity in a secular AI winner. The key insight: late in a macro selloff, high-conviction growth names with real fundamentals (not just narrative) tend to be bought aggressively on any stability signal. AMD had the AVGO earnings confirmation as an external catalyst. Conviction on AI infrastructure was rewarded.

trades.thesis: META at 613 after third consecutive weekly loss — still elevated at ~22x forward P/E with advertising revenue vulnerable to (1) economic slowdown reducing ad budgets, (2) tariff-driven consumer spending pullback hitting e-commerce advertisers, and (3) EU/global regulatory overhang. META's capex acceleration on AI data centers increases cash burn as revenue faces cyclical headwinds. Three-week losing streak suggests systematic selling not yet exhausted. Oil spike creating stagflation concerns that typically cause advertisers to pull back on discretionary campaigns.
trades.catalyst: Third consecutive weekly loss; SPY/QQQ in downtrend; Iran war stagflation fears; advertising revenue sensitivity to economic slowdown
trades.lesson: META bounced sharply from the late-March trough (~536) to 635 by Apr 13 — the recovery was faster and stronger than expected. The advertising revenue story proved more resilient than the macro sell thesis implied. META's own AI monetization (Llama, ad targeting improvements) offset the cyclical ad headwind. Shorting high-quality compounders after a multi-week selloff is dangerous — they tend to recover fastest when sentiment stabilizes.

period_summary.regime_notes: March 2-13 was a clear risk-off, tariff-shock + weak-labor double-punch period. The dominant profitable posture was SHORT broad market (SPY/QQQ) and SHORT consumer/growth stocks with tariff vulnerability (TSLA), combined with LONG energy (XOM) as an Iran-war / stagflation hedge. The two FLATs (GOOGL, TLT) reflect competing narratives — weak growth arguing for cuts offset inflation arguing against bonds. The losses (AVGO, IWM, META) all violated one key rule: in a genuine regime-shift selloff, macro overwhelms stock-specific and sector logic. AVGO had exceptional earnings; META was already sold off; IWM reflected delayed cut pricing — none mattered when systematic deleveraging was ongoing. The AMD LONG at day 8 of a 10-day selloff worked because it came late enough (valuation reset complete) with external confirmation (AVGO beat). Key lesson: in tariff-driven regime shifts, short breadth + long commodity inflation hedges, avoid individual stock longs until macro clears.
